CONSTRUCTION MARKETPLACE - POLICIES AND GUIDELINES

Document: Project Management Policies
Version: 2.1
Last Updated: January 2024

---

SECTION 1: PROJECT INITIATION

1.1 Contractor Onboarding Requirements
All contractors must complete a verification process before listing services on the platform.

rs must complete a verification process before listing services on the platform. This includes:
- Valid contractor license (state-issued)
- Proof of general liability insurance (minimum $1,000,000 per occurrence)
- Workers' compensation insurance for employees
- Background check completion within the last 2 years
- Minimum 3 verifiable references from prior projects

on within the last 2 years
- Minimum 3 verifiable references from prior projects

1.2 Project Listing Standards
Projects must include:
- Detailed scope of work description
- Estimated timeline with milestones
- Budget range with line-item breakdown
- Required permits and regulatory approvals
- Material specifications

---

SECTION 2: CONSTRUCTION PROJECT DELAYS

approvals
- Material specifications

---

SECTION 2: CONSTRUCTION PROJECT DELAYS

2.1 Common Causes of Project Delays
Construction project delays are one of the most significant challenges in the industry. The following factors are known to contribute to delays:

lenges in the industry. The following factors are known to contribute to delays:

Weather-Related Delays:
- Extreme weather conditions such as heavy rainfall, flooding, snow, or extreme heat can halt construction activities.
- Seasonal weather patterns must be factored into project scheduling.
- Force majeure clauses protect contractors from penalties during natural disasters.

rce majeure clauses protect contractors from penalties during natural disasters.

Supply Chain Issues:
- Shortage of construction materials (lumber, steel, concrete) can pause work.
- Global supply chain disruptions impact lead times for specialized equipment.
- Material price volatility can cause budget overruns leading to work stoppages.

- Material price volatility can cause budget overruns leading to work stoppages.

Labor Shortages:
- Skilled labor shortages in trades such as electricians, plumbers, and welders.
- High turnover rates among construction workers.
- Seasonal labor availability fluctuations.

Design and Planning Issues:
- Incomplete or unclear architectural/engineering drawings.
- Last-minute design changes requested by clients.
- Conflicts between structural, MEP (Mechanical, Electrical, Plumbing) designs.

- Conflicts between structural, MEP (Mechanical, Electrical, Plumbing) designs.

Permit and Regulatory Delays:
- Slow permit approval processes from municipal authorities.
- Unexpected code compliance issues discovered during inspections.
- Environmental compliance requirements and surveys.

Site Conditions:
- Unforeseen underground utilities or archaeological findings.
- Poor soil conditions requiring additional foundation work.
- Contaminated soil requiring remediation before construction.

foundation work.
- Contaminated soil requiring remediation before construction.

2.2 Delay Mitigation Strategies
- Conduct thorough pre-construction site assessments.
- Build buffer time (10-15%) into project schedules.
- Establish backup suppliers for critical materials.
- Maintain clear communication protocols between all parties.
- Use project management software to track milestones.

cols between all parties.
- Use project management software to track milestones.

2.3 Delay Penalty Clauses
Standard contracts on this platform include:
- Liquidated damages of $500-$2,000 per day for contractor-caused delays.
- No penalty for documented force majeure events.
- Client-caused delays entitle the contractor to time extensions and cost recovery.

---

SECTION 3: SAFETY STANDARDS

ntractor to time extensions and cost recovery.

---

SECTION 3: SAFETY STANDARDS

3.1 Mandatory Safety Requirements
All construction projects must comply with OSHA standards. Key requirements include:
- Personal Protective Equipment (PPE): Hard hats, safety vests, steel-toed boots are mandatory on all sites.
- Fall Protection: Required for work at heights above 6 feet.
- Scaffolding: Must be inspected daily and certified by a competent person.

eet.
- Scaffolding: Must be inspected daily and certified by a competent person.
- Electrical Safety: Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) procedures must be followed.
- Hazard Communication: All chemical hazards must be labeled and SDS (Safety Data Sheets) available.

3.2 Incident Reporting
- All injuries must be reported within 24 hours.
- OSHA recordable incidents require documentation and investigation.
- Near-miss events must be reported and reviewed.

cumentation and investigation.
- Near-miss events must be reported and reviewed.

3.3 Site Safety Inspections
- Weekly safety inspections are required.
- Third-party safety audits for projects exceeding $1 million.

---

SECTION 4: PAYMENT TERMS AND DISPUTES

4.1 Standard Payment Structure
The platform supports the following payment structures:
- Milestone-based payments (recommended for projects over $50,000)
- Percentage of completion payments
- Time and materials billing
- Fixed-price contracts

tage of completion payments
- Time and materials billing
- Fixed-price contracts

4.2 Payment Protection
- All payments are held in escrow until milestone approval.
- Disputes must be raised within 7 days of milestone completion.
- Platform mediation available for disputes under $10,000.
- Legal arbitration pathway for disputes exceeding $10,000.

putes under $10,000.
- Legal arbitration pathway for disputes exceeding $10,000.

4.3 Lien Rights
- Contractors retain right to file mechanic's liens for unpaid work.
- Platform requires lien waiver submission upon final payment.
- Sub-contractors must be paid within 7 days of general contractor receiving payment.

---

SECTION 5: QUALITY STANDARDS

days of general contractor receiving payment.

---

SECTION 5: QUALITY STANDARDS

5.1 Workmanship Standards
All work must meet:
- Local building codes and standards
- International Building Code (IBC) where applicable
- Manufacturer specifications for materials
- Industry best practices (ACI for concrete, AISC for steel)

tions for materials
- Industry best practices (ACI for concrete, AISC for steel)

5.2 Warranty Requirements
- Minimum 1-year workmanship warranty on all projects
- Material warranties pass through to the client
- Structural work carries a 10-year implied warranty under many state laws

5.3 Inspections and Sign-offs
- Rough-in inspections before concealing work
- Final inspections before project close-out
- Client walkthrough and punch list completion

pections before project close-out

CONSTRUCTION MARKETPLACE - FREQUENTLY ASKED QUESTIONS (FAQ)

Document: Platform FAQ
Version: 3.0
Last Updated: February 2024

---

Q1: How do I get started as a contractor on this platform?
A: To register as a contractor, follow these steps:
1. Create an account and complete your business profile.
2. Upload your contractor license, insurance certificates, and any relevant certifications.
3. Complete the identity verification process.
4. Submit three professional references.
5.

e the identity verification process.
4. Submit three professional references.
5. Once approved (typically 3-5 business days), you can start bidding on projects.

Q2: What types of construction projects are supported on the platform?
A: The platform supports a wide range of construction projects including:
- Residential construction (new homes, additions, renovations)
- Commercial construction (offices, retail spaces, warehouses)
- Industrial construction (factories, plants, infrastructure)
- Specialty work

- Industrial construction (factories, plants, infrastructure)
- Specialty work (electrical, plumbing, HVAC, roofing)
- Civil construction (roads, bridges, utilities)

Q3: How are contractor bids evaluated?
A: Bids are evaluated based on multiple criteria:
- Price competitiveness (40% weight)
- Contractor rating and reviews (30% weight)
- Relevant experience and past projects (20% weight)
- Timeline commitment (10% weight)
Project owners can adjust these criteria based on project priorities.

0% weight)
Project owners can adjust these criteria based on project priorities.

Q4: What happens if a contractor abandons a project?
A: If a contractor abandons a project:
- The remaining escrowed funds are released to the project owner.
- The contractor's rating is severely penalized.
- The platform may suspend or permanently ban repeat offenders.
- The project owner may seek damages through the platform's dispute resolution system.
- A substitute contractor listing is expedited.

orm's dispute resolution system.
- A substitute contractor listing is expedited.

Q5: How does the platform handle material procurement?
A: The platform offers two options:
- Owner-furnished materials: The project owner purchases materials directly.
- Contractor-furnished materials: The contractor procures materials, included in the contract price.
For contractor-furnished materials, receipts and invoices must be submitted for transparency.

r-furnished materials, receipts and invoices must be submitted for transparency.

Q6: What are the platform fees?
A: Platform fees are structured as follows:
- Project owners: Free to post projects; 2.5% transaction fee on payments processed.
- Contractors: 5% service fee on earned project revenue.
- Premium subscriptions available for enhanced visibility and features.

revenue.
- Premium subscriptions available for enhanced visibility and features.

Q7: How is project quality assured?
A: Quality assurance mechanisms include:
- Mandatory milestone inspections before payment release.
- Client review and rating system post-project completion.
- Third-party inspection services available for large projects.
- Dispute resolution team for quality complaints.

available for large projects.
- Dispute resolution team for quality complaints.

Q8: What insurance is required for projects?
A: Insurance requirements vary by project size:
- Projects under $50,000: General liability insurance ($500,000 minimum).
- Projects $50,000 - $500,000: General liability ($1,000,000) + workers' comp.
- Projects over $500,000: General liability ($2,000,000) + workers' comp + builder's risk insurance.

,000: General liability ($2,000,000) + workers' comp + builder's risk insurance.
- Specialty projects may require additional coverage (e.g., professional liability).

Q9: How are construction delays handled on the platform?
A: The platform has a structured delay management process:
- Contractors must report anticipated delays 48 hours in advance.
- Delay reasons are classified as contractor-caused, client-caused, or force majeure.

ay reasons are classified as contractor-caused, client-caused, or force majeure.
- Contractor-caused delays may trigger liquidated damages per contract terms.
- Clients can approve timeline extensions through the platform dashboard.
- All delay communications must be documented in the platform's messaging system.

All delay communications must be documented in the platform's messaging system.

Q10: Can subcontractors be used on projects?
A: Yes, with conditions:
- Primary contractors may subcontract up to 40% of the work value.
- All subcontractors must also be verified on the platform.
- Primary contractor remains fully responsible for subcontractor work quality.
- Subcontractors must be disclosed to the project owner before engagement.

lity.
- Subcontractors must be disclosed to the project owner before engagement.

Q11: What is the process for project change orders?
A: Change orders follow a formal process:
1. Contractor identifies scope change and prepares change order request.
2. Change order includes description, cost impact, and timeline impact.
3. Client reviews and approves/rejects through the platform.
4. Approved change orders become addendums to the original contract.
5.

latform.
4. Approved change orders become addendums to the original contract.
5. Payment for change orders follows the same escrow and milestone process.

Q12: How does the rating and review system work?
A: Both project owners and contractors receive ratings:
- Ratings are on a 1-5 star scale.
- Reviews are posted after project completion.
- Reviews are verified (only parties involved in the project can review).
- Dispute resolution team reviews complaints of fraudulent reviews.

can review).
- Dispute resolution team reviews complaints of fraudulent reviews.
- Consistently low-rated contractors (below 3.0) face account review.

Q13: What environmental regulations must contractors comply with?
A: Contractors must comply with applicable environmental regulations:
- Clean Water Act requirements for stormwater management.
- Clean Air Act compliance for dust and emission controls.
- Hazardous waste disposal per EPA regulations.
- Local environmental impact requirements.

s waste disposal per EPA regulations.
- Local environmental impact requirements.
- LEED certification requirements if specified in the project.

Q14: How is worker safety enforced on projects?
A: Worker safety is enforced through:
- Mandatory OSHA compliance certification for all contractors.
- Safety plans required for projects over $100,000.
- Random safety audits by platform-certified inspectors.
- Incident reporting integrated into the platform.

latform-certified inspectors.
- Incident reporting integrated into the platform.
- Contractors with poor safety records are subject to removal.

CONSTRUCTION MARKETPLACE - TECHNICAL SPECIFICATIONS

Document: Material and Technical Specifications
Version: 1.5
Last Updated: March 2024

---

SECTION A: CONCRETE SPECIFICATIONS

A1: General Concrete Requirements
- Minimum compressive strength: 3,000 PSI for residential, 4,000 PSI for commercial.
- Water-cement ratio: Maximum 0.45 for exposed surfaces, 0.50 for interior.
- Slump: 3-5 inches for standard pours, 4-7 inches for pumped concrete.
- Air entrainment: 5-7% for freeze-thaw environments.

nches for pumped concrete.
- Air entrainment: 5-7% for freeze-thaw environments.

A2: Concrete Curing Requirements
- Wet curing minimum 7 days for standard concrete.
- Temperature must remain above 50°F during curing.
- Curing compounds must meet ASTM C309 standards.
- High-strength concrete may require extended curing periods.

TM C309 standards.
- High-strength concrete may require extended curing periods.

A3: Reinforcement Requirements
- Rebar grade: Grade 60 (minimum) for structural applications.
- Concrete cover: 3 inches for footings, 2 inches for slabs, 1.5 inches for columns.
- Lap splice length: Minimum 40 bar diameters for tension splices.

---

SECTION B: STRUCTURAL STEEL SPECIFICATIONS

diameters for tension splices.

---

SECTION B: STRUCTURAL STEEL SPECIFICATIONS

B1: Steel Material Standards
- All structural steel must conform to ASTM A36 or A992.
- Bolts: ASTM A325 or A490 high-strength bolts for structural connections.
- Welding: AWS D1.1 Structural Welding Code compliance required.
- Surface preparation: SSPC-SP6 (commercial blast) minimum for painted steel.

d.
- Surface preparation: SSPC-SP6 (commercial blast) minimum for painted steel.

B2: Steel Fabrication Requirements
- Shop drawings required for all structural steel prior to fabrication.
- Mill certifications must accompany all steel deliveries.
- Non-destructive testing (NDT) for critical welds in high-occupancy structures.

---

SECTION C: FOUNDATION SPECIFICATIONS

l welds in high-occupancy structures.

---

SECTION C: FOUNDATION SPECIFICATIONS

C1: Soil Investigation Requirements
- Geotechnical investigation required for all commercial projects.
- Soil bearing capacity must be determined before foundation design.
- Expansive soils require special foundation design (drilled piers, post-tensioned slabs).

e soils require special foundation design (drilled piers, post-tensioned slabs).

C2: Foundation Types and Applications
Shallow Foundations:
- Spread footings: For well-bearing soil with capacity >1,500 psf.
- Mat foundations: For poor soil conditions or heavy loads.
- Minimum frost depth requirements vary by geographic location.

or heavy loads.
- Minimum frost depth requirements vary by geographic location.

Deep Foundations:
- Driven piles: Steel H-piles, precast concrete piles for deep bearing layers.
- Drilled piers (caissons): For large load concentrations.
- Micro-piles: For limited access sites or retrofit applications.

---

SECTION D: MECHANICAL, ELECTRICAL, PLUMBING (MEP) SPECIFICATIONS

ications.

---

SECTION D: MECHANICAL, ELECTRICAL, PLUMBING (MEP) SPECIFICATIONS

D1: HVAC Requirements
- Energy efficiency: Must meet ASHRAE 90.1 standards.
- Fresh air ventilation: Minimum 0.15 CFM per square foot per ASHRAE 62.1.
- Equipment access: Minimum 36-inch clearance for maintenance.
- Ductwork: Sheet metal per SMACNA standards; insulated to meet energy codes.

e.
- Ductwork: Sheet metal per SMACNA standards; insulated to meet energy codes.

D2: Electrical Requirements
- Service entrance: Sized per NEC Article 230.
- Grounding: Per NEC Article 250.
- GFCI protection: All kitchen, bath, garage, outdoor, and basement receptacles.
- AFCI protection: All bedroom circuits in residential construction.
- Commercial lighting: Must meet ASHRAE 90.1 lighting power density limits.

ion.
- Commercial lighting: Must meet ASHRAE 90.1 lighting power density limits.

D3: Plumbing Requirements
- Water supply: Minimum 45 PSI at fixtures, maximum 80 PSI.
- Hot water temperature: 120°F at fixtures for scalding prevention; 140°F at water heater.
- Drain, waste, vent: Per International Plumbing Code (IPC) or local equivalent.
- Backflow prevention: Required per local water authority standards.

---

SECTION E: ROOFING SPECIFICATIONS

red per local water authority standards.

---

SECTION E: ROOFING SPECIFICATIONS

E1: Sloped Roofing (>2:12 pitch)
- Asphalt shingles: Minimum 30-year architectural grade.
- Ice and water shield: Required at eaves, valleys, and penetrations.
- Underlayment: Synthetic minimum in all new construction.
- Metal roofing: Minimum 24-gauge; concealed fastener systems preferred.

uction.
- Metal roofing: Minimum 24-gauge; concealed fastener systems preferred.

E2: Low-Slope Roofing (<2:12 pitch)
- TPO membrane: Minimum 60-mil thickness; heat-welded seams.
- EPDM membrane: Minimum 60-mil; fully-adhered or ballasted.
- Modified bitumen: SBS or APP-modified; torch-down or cold-applied.
- Drainage: Maximum 1/4 inch per foot slope to drains.

rch-down or cold-applied.
- Drainage: Maximum 1/4 inch per foot slope to drains.

E3: Roofing Warranties
- Manufacturer product warranty: 20-30 years depending on system.
- Workmanship warranty: Minimum 2 years required by platform.
- NDL (No Dollar Limit) warranties available for qualifying systems.

---

SECTION F: SUSTAINABILITY AND GREEN BUILDING

lable for qualifying systems.

---

SECTION F: SUSTAINABILITY AND GREEN BUILDING

F1: Energy Efficiency Standards
- New construction: Must meet current IECC (International Energy Conservation Code).
- Commercial: ASHRAE 90.1 compliance required.
- Green building certifications accepted: LEED, ENERGY STAR, Green Globes.

ired.
- Green building certifications accepted: LEED, ENERGY STAR, Green Globes.

F2: Sustainable Materials
- Recycled content: Encouraged for steel (>25% recycled), carpet (>10%), concrete aggregate.
- Regional materials: Preference for materials sourced within 500 miles.
- Low-VOC: Required for interior paints, adhesives, sealants, and flooring.
- Certified wood: FSC-certified lumber preferred for wood framing.

and flooring.
- Certified wood: FSC-certified lumber preferred for wood framing.

F3: Water Conservation
- Low-flow fixtures: Required in all new construction.
- Irrigation efficiency: Drip irrigation and smart controllers for landscaping.
- Rainwater harvesting: Permitted in most jurisdictions; check local regulations.
- Greywater reuse: Allowed for irrigation in states with enabling legislation.

---

SECTION G: CONSTRUCTION WASTE MANAGEMENT

states with enabling legislation.

---

SECTION G: CONSTRUCTION WASTE MANAGEMENT

G1: Waste Diversion Requirements
- Projects over $500,000: Minimum 50% construction waste diversion from landfill.
- Separate waste streams required: Wood, metal, drywall, concrete, cardboard.
- Hazardous waste (lead paint, asbestos, PCBs): Licensed disposal contractors required.

dous waste (lead paint, asbestos, PCBs): Licensed disposal contractors required.

G2: Material Salvage
- Pre-demolition surveys recommended for salvage opportunities.
- Architectural salvage encouraged for doors, hardware, fixtures.
- Material exchanges and donations to Habitat for Humanity ReStores permitted.

.
- Material exchanges and donations to Habitat for Humanity ReStores permitted.
- Material exchanges and donations to Habitat for Humanity ReStores permitted.
- Material exchanges and donations to Habitat for Humanity ReStores permitted.
